subtopic_detail: Empiric oral antibiotic for first febrile UTI in well toddler
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing an 18-month-old girl in the short-stay unit with 24 hours of fever and reduced feeding. She is alert with no rash and tolerating sips. A clean-catch urine collected after meatal cleaning shows leucocyte esterase positive, nitrites positive, and microscopy with 200 white cells per microlitre. Local antibiogram shows E. coli trimethoprim resistance of 28 percent and cefalexin resistance of 6 percent.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent, BP 96/58
data_table.Examination: Alert, hydrated, soft non-tender abdomen, no flank tenderness, normal external genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Urine LE positive, nitrites positive, WCC 200/microL, urine culture pending; venous blood gas pH 7.38, lactate 1.4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric oral antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Amoxicillin clavulanate has broader gram negative cover than needed and higher rates of diarrhoea compared with the first-line agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until urine culture sensitivities are reported tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Delay in treatment of confirmed febrile UTI increases the risk of renal scarring; empiric therapy is started after the urine sample is taken. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 4 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Trimethoprim resistance of 28 percent locally exceeds the 20 percent ceiling for reliable empiric cover in febrile UTI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 7 days starting now
options.rationale: Cefalexin is the AU first-line oral agent for well children over 6 months with uncomplicated upper UTI given local resistance patterns and reliable oral absorption. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous gentamicin 7 mg/kg daily admit for 48 hours then oral switch
options.rationale: Intravenous therapy is reserved for children who are unwell, vomiting, under 3 months, or unable to tolerate oral feeds; this child is well enough for oral therapy. (Source: RCH CPG)

explanation.summary: Febrile UTI in young children is treated empirically after urine collection; the choice of oral agent depends on local resistance patterns and oral tolerance. AU practice favours cefalexin as a first-line agent when local E. coli resistance to trimethoprim is above the 20 percent ceiling. A 7 to 10 day oral course is standard for upper tract UTI in well children. Renal ultrasound is recommended after the first febrile UTI in children under 6 months and after atypical or recurrent infections in older children.
key_points: Empiric oral cefalexin first-line when trimethoprim resistance over 20 percent
IV therapy reserved for unwell, under 3 months, or vomiting
Renal ultrasound after first febrile UTI under 6 months or atypical features
Do not delay empirics for culture in symptomatic confirmed UTI
explanation.pearls: In Australian antibiograms most paediatric units now exceed the 20 percent trimethoprim resistance threshold, making oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg twice daily the default empiric for well febrile UTI.

subtopic_detail: Postexposure prophylaxis for household contacts of confirmed pertussis
stem: You are the GP registrar seeing a family after their 7-year-old son was diagnosed with PCR-confirmed pertussis 2 days ago. The household includes the index case, his 32-year-old mother who is 34 weeks pregnant, his 38-year-old father, an 8-week-old sister who is fully vaccinated for age, and a 4-year-old brother whose immunisations are up to date.

data_table.Vital signs: All household members afebrile and asymptomatic at presentation today
data_table.Examination: Index case isolating at home with paroxysmal cough; other family members no respiratory symptoms
data_table.Investigations: Index case pertussis PCR positive 2 days ago; other family members no testing performed

lead_in: Which household members should receive antibiotic postexposure prophylaxis today?

options.text: Only the 8-week-old sister receives azithromycin while other contacts are observed at home
options.rationale: Selective prophylaxis to the infant alone leaves household transmission via the other contacts unaddressed during the infectious period. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

options.text: No prophylaxis is given because all household contacts are currently asymptomatic on review
options.rationale: Pertussis transmits from up to 6 days before cough onset; absence of symptoms in contacts does not exclude infection or transmission risk. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

options.text: Mother, 8-week-old sister, 4-year-old brother, and father all receive azithromycin
options.rationale: AU guidance recommends prophylaxis for all household contacts when a high-risk contact is present such as a young infant or a third-trimester pregnant woman. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

options.text: Only the mother and 8-week-old sister receive azithromycin while other contacts are observed
options.rationale: Restricting prophylaxis omits the father and 4-year-old brother who can transmit to both high-risk contacts within the household. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

options.text: All household contacts receive trimethoprim sulfamethoxazole as the preferred first-line agent
options.rationale: Trimethoprim sulfamethoxazole is a second-line agent in pertussis prophylaxis reserved for macrolide-intolerant individuals over 2 months of age. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Pertussis postexposure prophylaxis aims to interrupt household transmission and protect high-risk contacts. AU CDNA SoNG defines high-risk contacts as infants under 6 months, women in the last month of pregnancy, and unvaccinated childcare workers. When a high-risk contact is identified all household members receive prophylaxis regardless of immunisation status. Azithromycin is the macrolide of choice for infants over 1 month due to lower hypertrophic pyloric stenosis risk than erythromycin.
key_points: Azithromycin first-line for all ages including infants over 1 month
All household contacts treated if any high-risk contact present
High-risk: infant under 6 months, last month of pregnancy
Trimethoprim sulfamethoxazole only if macrolide-intolerant and over 2 months
explanation.pearls: Maternal pertussis vaccination in pregnancy from 20 weeks provides passive protection but does not eliminate the need for household prophylaxis when a confirmed case is identified.

subtopic_detail: Pharmacotherapy choice for moderate croup in ED
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 2-year-old boy with a 12-hour history of barking cough, hoarseness, and intermittent stridor at rest. He is alert and accepts oral medications. There is no drooling, history of foreign body, or recent vaccinations missed. Local supply of dexamethasone oral solution is currently available.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8, HR 138, RR 36, SpO2 97 percent on air
data_table.Examination: Barking cough, stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, alert, normal air entry bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Westley croup score 4 (moderate); no investigations indicated for typical croup
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol given by parents 4 hours ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Nebulised budesonide 2 mg as initial inhaled corticosteroid because oral intake is uncertain
options.rationale: Budesonide is reserved for children unable to tolerate oral steroid; this child is alert and accepting oral medications. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose now and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: AU practice supports a single oral dexamethasone dose of 0.15 mg/kg for moderate croup with onset of benefit within 30 to 60 minutes. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer and observation pending resolution of stridor
options.rationale: Salbutamol does not treat the subglottic oedema responsible for croup and delays definitive steroid therapy. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 alone now without corticosteroid
options.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for severe distress as it acts for 1 to 2 hours and requires steroid co-administration to avoid rebound. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg single dose tried first because dexamethasone is unavailable
options.rationale: Dexamethasone is the preferred AU first-line agent due to longer half-life and single-dose efficacy; prednisolone is an alternative only if dexamethasone is unavailable. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup is treated according to a severity score with oral dexamethasone the AU first-line for mild to moderate cases. Stridor at rest with mild recession indicates moderate severity warranting steroid and observation but not adrenaline. Nebulised adrenaline is added for severe disease with marked recession, cyanosis, or altered mental state and always with concurrent steroid. Disposition depends on response at 4 hours after steroid: stable children can usually go home.
key_points: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose first-line
Adrenaline only for severe distress and always with steroid
Budesonide nebulised only if oral intake not possible
Discharge after 4 hours if no stridor at rest
explanation.pearls: Onset of benefit from oral dexamethasone in croup is 30 to 60 minutes with peak at 6 hours; this matches the typical 4-hour ED observation window before safe discharge.

subtopic_detail: IM adrenaline weight-band dosing and route in school-age child with anaphylaxis
stem: You are the ED registrar called to resuscitation as an 8-year-old boy arrives by ambulance after eating a peanut snack at school 20 minutes ago. He developed lip swelling, generalised urticaria, then dyspnoea and one episode of vomiting. Paramedics administered 300 micrograms intramuscular adrenaline 10 minutes ago via his school auto-injector. He weighs 32 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 154, RR 38, SpO2 91 percent on 6 L/min oxygen, BP 78/42
data_table.Examination: Widespread urticaria, perioral swelling, audible wheeze bilaterally, prolonged capillary refill, anxious
data_table.Medications: Adrenaline 300 micrograms IM 10 minutes ago (auto-injector); now 20 minutes since exposure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next adrenaline order while you secure IV access and prepare a fluid bolus?

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 as the next adrenaline dose for upper airway oedema
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is an adjunct for stridor but does not address shock or lower airway anaphylaxis and does not replace systemic IM dosing. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 300 micrograms in the deltoid as a second dose for ongoing wheeze and hypotension
options.rationale: Subcutaneous absorption is unreliable during shock; intramuscular anterolateral thigh is the recommended route in anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 150 micrograms in the anterolateral thigh as the appropriate paediatric weight-band dose
options.rationale: The 150 microgram dose corresponds to children between 10 and 20 kg and underdoses a 32 kg child experiencing persistent severe anaphylaxis. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg push as a single bolus over 30 seconds while preparing fluid
options.rationale: IV bolus adrenaline outside cardiac arrest risks malignant arrhythmia and hypertensive crisis and is reserved for refractory shock as an infusion. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 300 micrograms in the anterolateral thigh now and repeat at 5 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: AU ASCIA guidance prescribes 10 micrograms/kg IM up to 500 micrograms with repeat dosing every 5 minutes for persistent severe anaphylaxis; in a 32 kg child the rounded dose is 300 micrograms IM. (Source: ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis treatment in children is repeated IM adrenaline in the anterolateral thigh every 5 minutes for persistent severe features. The AU ASCIA weight bands prescribe 150 micrograms for 10 to 20 kg, 300 micrograms for 20 to 50 kg, and 500 micrograms for over 50 kg with simultaneous fluid resuscitation. Refractory shock after 2 or 3 IM doses prompts an adrenaline infusion at 0.1 to 1 microgram/kg/min titrated to response with continuous cardiac monitoring. Antihistamines and steroids are not first-line in acute anaphylaxis.
key_points: IM adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg up to 500 micrograms every 5 minutes
Weight bands: 150 for 10-20 kg, 300 for 20-50 kg, 500 for over 50 kg
IV infusion only when refractory after 2-3 IM doses
Fluids 20 mL/kg crystalloid alongside adrenaline for shock
explanation.pearls: Persistent shock after 2 IM doses indicates impending refractory anaphylaxis; commence an adrenaline infusion early at 0.1 microgram/kg/min and call for senior airway support rather than escalating IM dose.

subtopic_detail: Empiric antibiotic and LP timing in late onset suspected neonatal sepsis
stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 12-day-old term boy brought in by his mother with poor feeding and reduced activity for 8 hours. He is now lethargic and grunting. He was born by uncomplicated vaginal delivery, breastfed, and discharged at 36 hours. He has not received any maternal antibiotics in labour. The local cefotaxime supply is unavailable due to a national shortage and ceftriaxone is being used as the substitute despite the patient being a term neonate without jaundice.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 192, RR 68, SpO2 94 percent, BP 62/38, prolonged capillary refill
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, grunting, mottled, anterior fontanelle full, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Venous gas pH 7.22 base excess -10, glucose 2.6, lactate 4.2, WCC 22, CRP 78, blood culture taken

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next set of antimicrobial actions?

options.text: Commence IV cefazolin and gentamicin then add aciclovir if seizures or focal CNS signs develop later
options.rationale: Cefazolin lacks reliable CNS penetration; aciclovir is added empirically in any late-onset neonate with sepsis features even without seizures. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis in the neonate

options.text: Commence IV vancomycin and meropenem to cover resistant gram negatives and methicillin resistant staph
options.rationale: Broad cover with vancomycin and meropenem is reserved for nosocomial late-onset sepsis or proven resistance; community late-onset is treated with first-line regimen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin alone given a term neonate with cefotaxime unavailable on site
options.rationale: Late-onset sepsis with shock and full fontanelle requires meningitic third-generation cephalosporin cover not only beta-lactam plus aminoglycoside. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis in the neonate

options.text: Start IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin and ceftriaxone immediately then proceed to lumbar puncture when stabilised
options.rationale: Late-onset neonatal sepsis with septic shock requires immediate triple antimicrobial cover with meningitic ceftriaxone dose; LP is deferred until cardiovascular stability is achieved. (Source: RCH CPG Sepsis; ANZCOR)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis in the neonate

options.text: Perform lumbar puncture first then commence IV benzylpenicillin and gentamicin only after CSF is obtained
options.rationale: LP must not delay antibiotics in a shocked neonate; antibiotics are given immediately after blood cultures and LP is performed when safe. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis in the neonate

explanation.summary: Late-onset neonatal sepsis in a community setting is empirically covered with benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin and a third-generation cephalosporin at meningitic dose to cover group B streptococcus, E. coli, and Listeria with CNS penetration. Aciclovir is added if features suggest possible HSV. Antibiotics must not be delayed for lumbar puncture in a haemodynamically unstable infant; the LP is performed when the patient is stabilised. Ceftriaxone may be used in term, non-jaundiced neonates over 1 week when cefotaxime is unavailable.
key_points: Late-onset community sepsis: benzylpenicillin + gentamicin + cefotaxime or ceftriaxone
Add aciclovir empirically in late-onset neonatal sepsis with CNS features
Antibiotics first, LP when stable
Ceftriaxone acceptable substitute in term non-jaundiced neonates
explanation.pearls: Maternal HSV history is often absent in neonatal disseminated herpes; empiric aciclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours in a septic neonate has minimal downside and prevents catastrophic delay.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Sepsis in the neonate

subtopic_detail: Pharmacotherapy choice for hyperemesis gravidarum in early pregnancy
stem: You are the antenatal day-unit registrar reviewing a 28-year-old primigravida at 9 weeks gestation with persistent vomiting unable to tolerate oral fluids for 36 hours. She has lost 4 kg from booking weight. She has not tried any antiemetic so far. There is no history of liver disease and no abdominal pain. She has been managing on diet modification and ginger which are now ineffective.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 108, BP 102/68 lying, postural drop 18 mmHg, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, no abdominal tenderness, uterus non-palpable
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 4 plus, urine specific gravity 1.030, sodium 134, potassium 3.1, urea 7.4, creatinine 82, ALT 38, TSH 0.6
data_table.Medications: Nil regular; folate 5 mg daily and pregnancy multivitamin

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Metoclopramide 10 mg IV three times daily without oral antiemetic until tolerating diet again
options.rationale: Metoclopramide is second-line; first-line is pyridoxine plus doxylamine before stepping up to dopamine antagonists. (Source: RANZCOG)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily and doxylamine 12.5 mg up to four times daily with IV fluids
options.rationale: AU practice initiates pyridoxine plus doxylamine as the first-line antiemetic combination in pregnancy for women who have failed lifestyle measures. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Methylprednisolone 16 mg three times daily orally as the appropriate step in refractory presentation
options.rationale: Systemic corticosteroids are a last-line option after multiple antiemetics have failed and require senior obstetric agreement. (Source: RANZCOG)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Ondansetron 4 mg IV every 8 hours as the preferred initial antiemetic given the severity of symptoms
options.rationale: Ondansetron is reserved for second-line use after pyridoxine doxylamine and metoclopramide fail in AU due to possible association with orofacial clefts. (Source: RANZCOG)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

options.text: Prochlorperazine 12.5 mg IM as a single dose then plan oral antiemetic on discharge from the unit
options.rationale: Phenothiazines are second-line and have a greater extrapyramidal side-effect burden in early pregnancy than the first-line combination. (Source: AMH; RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: AU first-line for nausea and vomiting in pregnancy refractory to lifestyle measures is pyridoxine plus doxylamine. Second-line options include metoclopramide and prochlorperazine; ondansetron is reserved for failures of these due to uncertain safety signals in the first trimester. Hyperemesis with ketonuria, weight loss over 5 percent, and electrolyte disturbance warrants IV fluid resuscitation, thiamine cover before glucose, and admission if oral hydration cannot be re-established. Corticosteroids are last-line.
key_points: Pyridoxine plus doxylamine is first-line in AU
Ondansetron second or third line due to cleft signal
Thiamine before glucose IV in prolonged vomiting
Steroids last-line under senior obstetric input
explanation.pearls: In refractory hyperemesis with prolonged vomiting beyond 3 weeks, IV thiamine 100 mg before glucose-containing fluids prevents Wernicke encephalopathy that has been reported in pregnancy.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and Vomiting in Pregnancy

subtopic_detail: GBS intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis with severe penicillin allergy
stem: You are the obstetric registrar admitting a 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks gestation in early labour with intact membranes. Her routine vaginal-rectal swab at 36 weeks grew group B streptococcus. She reports a documented episode of laryngeal oedema and hypotension when given amoxicillin in childhood. Sensitivities on the swab show clindamycin resistance but vancomycin susceptibility.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal T 36.8, HR 84, BP 118/72; fetal heart rate baseline 138 with normal variability
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, head at -1 station, intact membranes
data_table.Investigations: GBS positive 36 weeks (clindamycin resistant, vancomycin susceptible); FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intrapartum antibiotic regimen?

options.text: IV vancomycin 20 mg/kg every 8 hours starting now and until delivery as appropriate IAP
options.rationale: Severe penicillin anaphylaxis with clindamycin resistance leaves vancomycin as the recommended IAP option in AU practice. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Guidelines)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 500 mg twice daily as the practical option while awaiting IV access placement
options.rationale: Oral macrolides are not appropriate for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis due to inadequate fetal levels and poor evidence base. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: IM benzathine penicillin 2.4 million units as a single dose to cover the expected duration of labour
options.rationale: IM benzathine penicillin is contraindicated in confirmed penicillin anaphylaxis and is not the standard intrapartum regimen. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: IV clindamycin 900 mg every 8 hours starting now as the recommended next-line beta-lactam alternative
options.rationale: Documented clindamycin resistance on swab means it cannot be relied upon for adequate fetal cover during labour. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: IV cefazolin 2 g every 8 hours starting now noting the absence of severe penicillin allergy features
options.rationale: A clear history of anaphylaxis to a beta-lactam contraindicates cefazolin even though cross-reactivity is low overall. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for GBS-positive women in AU is benzylpenicillin first-line with a 4 hour interval before delivery preferred. Cefazolin is the alternative in mild non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy. Severe allergy with anaphylaxis features requires clindamycin if susceptibility is confirmed or vancomycin if resistance or unknown susceptibility. Inadequate IAP cover prompts neonatal observation per the local pathway.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin first-line for GBS IAP
Cefazolin if mild non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy
Vancomycin if anaphylaxis and clindamycin resistant
At least 4 hours pre-delivery for adequate cover
explanation.pearls: A confirmed past history of anaphylaxis to any beta-lactam is treated as severe penicillin allergy for GBS IAP planning even if the reaction predates allergy clinic testing.

subtopic_detail: Initial management plan for PPROM at 33 weeks without infection
stem: You are the obstetric registrar admitting a 29-year-old G2P1 at 33+0 weeks gestation with a clear gush of fluid 6 hours ago and ongoing leakage confirmed by sterile speculum pooling. She is afebrile and not contracting. Cardiotocography shows a reassuring trace. She has no history of penicillin allergy and the GBS swab from 28 weeks was negative.

data_table.Examination: Sterile speculum: pooling, fern positive; closed cervix; uterus non-tender
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, CRP 6, MSU clear, ultrasound presenting cephalic, EFW 2050 g, AFI 4 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Admit and plan immediate induction of labour given prolonged rupture risk and a viable fetal weight estimate
options.rationale: Routine immediate delivery at 33 weeks is not supported by current AU practice when both mother and fetus are stable; expectant management to 34 weeks reduces neonatal respiratory morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Admit and start broad-spectrum amoxicillin clavulanate antibiotic prophylaxis with corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation
options.rationale: Amoxicillin clavulanate is avoided in PPROM due to a signal of increased neonatal necrotising enterocolitis from clavulanate. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Admit, start IV erythromycin 250 mg every 6 hours, give betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, and plan expectant management until 34 weeks if stable
options.rationale: AU practice for PPROM between 24 and 34 weeks is expectant management with erythromycin antibiotic prophylaxis and a single corticosteroid course aiming for delivery at 34 weeks if stable. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM)

options.text: Admit and commence magnesium sulfate neuroprotection without antibiotic prophylaxis or corticosteroid administration
options.rationale: Magnesium neuroprotection is added when delivery is anticipated within 24 hours below 30 weeks; antibiotics and steroids remain core elements of PPROM care. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Discharge home with twice weekly review and oral erythromycin if asymptomatic given the reassuring cardiotocograph today
options.rationale: Outpatient management of PPROM is not standard in AU due to risk of chorioamnionitis and cord prolapse; admission is recommended. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes between 24 and 34 weeks is managed expectantly to maximise fetal lung maturity if there is no evidence of infection, abruption, or non-reassuring fetal status. Erythromycin for 10 days reduces neonatal infection risk; amoxicillin clavulanate is avoided due to NEC risk. A single course of antenatal corticosteroids accelerates lung maturity. Magnesium neuroprotection is added if delivery is anticipated below 30 weeks gestation.
key_points: Expectant management to 34 weeks if stable
Erythromycin 10 days, avoid clavulanate
Single course antenatal corticosteroids 24 to 34+6
Magnesium neuroprotection if delivery imminent below 30 weeks
explanation.pearls: AFI under 5 cm in PPROM is associated with shorter latency to delivery and higher risk of chorioamnionitis; document daily fetal heart rate, maternal observations, and weekly inflammatory markers to detect occult infection.

subtopic_detail: Management of oncogenic HPV not 16/18 with reflex LBC LSIL on routine cervical screen
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 39-year-old woman with a routine cervical screening test today. She has no symptoms, no postcoital bleeding, and her last screen 5 years ago was negative. She is well, has two children, and has had a Mirena for the last 2 years for menorrhagia.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, vagina healthy, cervix appears normal at speculum
data_table.Investigations: Today: oncogenic HPV detected (not 16 or 18). Reflex liquid-based cytology: low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion. No prior abnormal screening.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 5 years given the long interval and her absence of any symptoms
options.rationale: An oncogenic HPV result on routine screen always requires earlier than 5 year follow-up under the AU NCSP. (Source: NCSP)

options.text: Repeat cervical screening test in 12 months as per the AU National Cervical Screening Program protocol
options.rationale: Oncogenic HPV not 16/18 with reflex LBC of LSIL or lower is managed by a repeat screen at 12 months under the AU NCSP. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to colposcopy within 8 weeks for evaluation of low-grade cytology in an HPV-positive woman over 30
options.rationale: Colposcopy is reserved for oncogenic HPV 16/18 detection or high-grade cytology, not for LSIL with non 16/18 HPV at first detection. (Source: NCSP)

options.text: Arrange large loop excision of the transformation zone for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes
options.rationale: LLETZ is performed after biopsy-confirmed high-grade dysplasia and is not indicated for LSIL with non 16/18 HPV. (Source: NCSP)

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 6 months because she has a long-acting intrauterine device in place
options.rationale: Mirena does not alter the NCSP follow-up interval; the standard 12 month repeat applies. (Source: NCSP)

explanation.summary: Under the AU NCSP, the routine cervical screening test is performed every 5 years from age 25 to 74. Detection of oncogenic HPV other than 16/18 with reflex cytology of low-grade or negative triggers a repeat in 12 months; oncogenic HPV 16/18 triggers colposcopy regardless of cytology. High-grade cytology triggers colposcopy. Routine screening from age 25 is performed via self-collection or clinician-collected swab.
key_points: Oncogenic HPV not 16/18 plus LSIL or negative LBC: repeat in 12 months
Oncogenic HPV 16/18: refer to colposcopy
High-grade cytology: refer to colposcopy regardless of HPV type
Self-collection is equivalent to clinician collection from age 25
explanation.pearls: Self-collected samples are now accepted across the AU NCSP and have equivalent sensitivity for HPV detection; offer this option to women who decline a speculum examination.

subtopic_detail: Timing and counselling for Mirena insertion for heavy menstrual bleeding
stem: You are the GP registrar seeing a 41-year-old woman with 6 months of heavy menstrual bleeding flooding through tampons every hour for two days per cycle. She has no postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding. Pelvic ultrasound is unremarkable with a 6 mm endometrial thickness on day 5 and no fibroids. Her ferritin is 8 micrograms/L and she has commenced oral iron. She is in a stable relationship and does not desire further pregnancy and has chosen the Mirena.

data_table.Examination: BMI 26; mobile, anteverted uterus; no adnexal masses
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 96, ferritin 8; pelvic ultrasound day 5 endometrium 6 mm, no fibroids; cervical screening up to date
data_table.Medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily started today

lead_in: What is the most appropriate plan for Mirena insertion?

options.text: Insert only after 6 weeks of oral iron therapy to allow haemoglobin to fully recover before any procedure
options.rationale: Heavy menstrual bleeding is itself an indication to insert sooner rather than later to control ongoing blood loss while iron therapy continues. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Insert after a 3 month trial of tranexamic acid alone given that hormonal devices are second-line for menorrhagia in AU practice
options.rationale: Mirena is recommended as first-line treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding in AU; tranexamic acid is an alternative if hormonal options are declined. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Insert at any time in her cycle today if pregnancy can be confidently excluded and start contraceptive cover immediately
options.rationale: AU Mirena guidance supports quick-start insertion at any time in the cycle once pregnancy is excluded with immediate contraceptive efficacy if inserted within the first 7 days of menses. (Source: FSRH; RANZCOG)

options.text: Insert after endometrial biopsy because all women over 40 with heavy menstrual bleeding require histological sampling first
options.rationale: Endometrial sampling is indicated for postmenopausal bleeding, persistent intermenstrual bleeding, or failed initial therapy not for first-line LARC insertion in this scenario. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Insert only during days 1 to 7 of menses because this is the only time pregnancy can be excluded reliably
options.rationale: Quick-start at any cycle day is acceptable once pregnancy is reasonably excluded; restricting to menses delays effective contraception. (Source: ASHA)

explanation.summary: Mirena is AU first-line for heavy menstrual bleeding once structural pathology is excluded and provides effective contraception. Quick-start insertion at any cycle day is acceptable provided pregnancy can be excluded; insertion within the first 7 days of menses provides immediate contraceptive cover, otherwise barrier protection for 7 days. Endometrial sampling is reserved for persistent abnormal bleeding patterns or failed medical management.
key_points: Mirena first-line for heavy menstrual bleeding
Quick-start any cycle day with pregnancy exclusion
Add condom for 7 days if inserted after day 7
Biopsy not required before insertion for typical heavy menses
explanation.pearls: Up to 65 percent of women experience amenorrhoea by 12 months with Mirena, which often resolves the heavy menstrual bleeding without further intervention even when fibroids are present.
